verses.text: And it came to pass, when David and his men were come to Ziklag on the third day, that the Amalekites had invaded the south, and Ziklag, and smitten Ziklag, and burned it with fire; And had taken the women captives, that were therein: they slew not any, either great or small, but carried them away, and went on their way. Guidance: Three days into the return march from Aphek, David and his six hundred arrive at their home base and find smoke. The Amalekites — David's old raid targets from 27:8 — have taken advantage of his absence to retaliate. Ziklag is on the southern frontier, the kind of town a Bedouin force can hit and disappear from in twenty-four hours. The narrator gives us one piece of providential mercy buried in the catastrophe: 'they slew not any, either great or small, but carried them away.' The Amalekites took captives, not lives. Probably they intended to sell the women and children into the slave markets of Egypt or coastal Philistia — a more lucrative outcome than execution. Either way, the women and children of David's town are alive when David arrives at the smoking gate. The detail will matter: David is going to be told to pursue and recover, not to mourn the dead. God's mercies are sometimes hidden inside the worst day of one's life as a single fact that makes the next sentence possible. The captives are alive. That is the door.

And it came to pass, when David and his men were come to Ziklag on the third day, that the Amalekites had invaded the south, and Ziklag, and smitten Ziklag, and burned it with fire; And had taken the women captives, that were therein: they slew not any, either great or small, but carried them away, and went on their way. Guidance: Three days into the return march from Aphek, David and his six hundred arrive at their home base and find smoke. So David and his men came to the city, and, behold, it was burned with fire; and their wives, and their sons, and their daughters, were taken captives. Then David and the people that were with him lifted up their voice and wept, until they had no more power to weep. And David's two wives were taken captives, Ahinoam the Jezreelitess, and Abigail the wife of Nabal the Carmelite. Guidance: The first response is collapse. 'David and the people that were with him lifted up their voice and wept, until they had no more power to weep.' The Hebrew phrase 'until they had no more power to weep' is one of the most physically realistic depictions of grief in scripture — they cried until their bodies could not produce another sob. Six hundred grown warriors weeping in the dust of a burned town, every man missing wife and children. The narrator does not editorialize. Then the personal note: David's two wives — Ahinoam and Abigail (the latter named, for the last time in scripture, as Nabal's widow, a quiet reminder of how she came to David) — are among the captives. The chapter is making us feel that David is not in a different category from his men. The king-to-be loses what his men lose; he weeps the same tears. Leadership in this moment is grief alongside, not above. The crown is going to require him to do something else in the next verse, but first he has to be a man whose wives have been taken. Pretending otherwise would have made him useless as a leader in the days ahead.

And David was greatly distressed; for the people spake of stoning him, because the soul of all the people was grieved, every man for his sons and for his daughters: but David encouraged himself in the LORD his God. Guidance: The grief turns to mutiny. The men, looking for someone to blame, decide it is David — he was the one who marched them off with Achish, leaving Ziklag undefended; his sixteen-month Philistine residency had put their families in the position of being preyed upon by raiders they could not retaliate against. The men 'spake of stoning him.' This is the lowest point of David's wilderness years. The same six hundred who followed him out of Adullam through caves and chases and a humiliating Philistine residency are now ready to murder him with rocks. The narrator's verdict on the moment is one of the most quoted phrases in scripture: 'David encouraged himself in the LORD his God' (Hebrew: vayyitchazek David ba-YHWH elohav). The verb chazaq is reflexive — strengthened himself. It is the same root the same narrator used of Jonathan strengthening David's hand in God at the wood of Ziph (23:16); now David has to do for himself what Jonathan once did for him. There is no prophet here, no friend, no encouragement-from-outside; only the LORD and David's own deliberate act of finding strength in Him. Sometimes faith is forced into the most personal posture: a man alone, his community against him, his family taken, his town burned — and the only resource left is the LORD. He does not deny the disaster; he 'strengthens himself' in the One whose covenant has not been canceled by it. This single line is the spiritual key to everything David does in the rest of the chapter. He acts because he has been strengthened first. Action without strengthening would have been desperation; strengthening without action would have been quietism. The combination is what God's people are for.

And David said to Abiathar the priest, Ahimelech's son, I pray thee, bring me hither the ephod. And Abiathar brought thither the ephod to David. And David enquired at the LORD, saying, Shall I pursue after this troop? shall I overtake them? And he answered him, Pursue: for thou shalt surely overtake them, and without fail recover all. Guidance: The first concrete act after self-strengthening is inquiry. 'I pray thee, bring me hither the ephod.' The same ephod that has been at David's side since chapter 22, the same priestly oracle David used at Keilah (23:9-12), the same Abiathar who escaped Doeg's massacre. The instrument has been sitting unused throughout the sixteen-month Philistine compromise (David didn't inquire about going to Achish in chapter 27, didn't inquire about anything else recorded). Now, with everything taken from him, David remembers that the ephod is for asking. The two questions are precise. 'Shall I pursue?' (is the pursuit God's will?) 'Shall I overtake?' (will the pursuit actually catch them, given the Amalekites' lead time?) The LORD's answer is more than David asked: 'Pursue: for thou shalt surely overtake them, and without fail recover all.' Two questions answered with three promises. Pursue. Overtake. Recover all. The promise of total restoration is the engine of the rest of the chapter. God adds the third clause to a man whose primary concern was the first two. There are days when God answers more than we ask, because He sees what we don't yet know to want.

So David went, he and the six hundred men that were with him, and came to the brook Besor, where those that were left behind stayed. David pursued, he and four hundred men: for two hundred abode behind, which were so faint that they could not go over the brook Besor. Guidance: The pursuit begins immediately. Six hundred men, exhausted from the three-day march from Aphek and broken by the day's news, set out southeast at speed. They reach the brook Besor — probably the wadi Ghazzeh, the largest seasonal stream in the western Negev — and two hundred of them collapse. 'So faint that they could not go over the brook Besor.' The Hebrew piggrim refers to those too exhausted to continue. After three days of forced march followed by the day of grief, two-thirds of the men can still go; one third cannot. David does not punish them; he simply leaves them with the supplies and takes the four hundred who can keep going. The episode is going to matter later — the two hundred who stay behind are going to be sidelined by the angry hard-line element when the spoils are divided. The chapter is setting up the question of how the recovered abundance will be shared among those who fought and those who guarded the rear. The wilderness of Besor is the rest-station God provides for the body that has reached its limit; sometimes the most honoring thing a leader can do is let half his force be honest about being unable to continue.

And they found an Egyptian in the field, and brought him to David, and gave him bread, and he did eat; and they made him drink water; And they gave him a piece of a cake of figs, and two clusters of raisins: and when he had eaten, his spirit came again to him: for he had eaten no bread, nor drunk any water, three days and three nights. Guidance: The pursuit is into wilderness south of Ziklag, with no road system to follow. By all odds, David's four hundred should never find a Bedouin raiding party that moves faster than they do and has a day's head start. Then, in a field, they come across an Egyptian — alone, dying. The narrator gives us the medical detail: 'he had eaten no bread, nor drunk any water, three days and three nights.' He is hours from death from dehydration in the Negev sun. David's men give him bread and water immediately, then add figs and raisins for blood sugar. 'When he had eaten, his spirit came again to him.' The verb naphsho is literally 'his soul returned' — he revived from the brink. The detail is medically accurate; sugar revives the desperately starved faster than bread does. David's men do not yet know who he is; they treat him as a fellow human in distress before they extract intelligence from him. The order of operations is the chapter's quiet morality: feed first, ask second. Mercy precedes utility. The same band that has been wronged and is hunting their wrongdoers stops to revive a stranger they could have left to die. The chapter is showing us that even pursuing what was stolen, David's men retain the capacity for unforced kindness. And that capacity is exactly what God will use to redirect the entire pursuit. The man they nearly stepped over is the GPS to the Amalekite camp.

And David said unto him, To whom belongest thou? and whence art thou? And he said, I am a young man of Egypt, servant to an Amalekite; and my master left me, because three days agone I fell sick. We made an invasion upon the south of the Cherethites, and upon the coast which belongeth to Judah, and upon the south of Caleb; and we burned Ziklag with fire. And David said to him, Canst thou bring me down to this company? And he said, Swear unto me by God, that thou wilt neither kill me, nor deliver me into the hands of my master, and I will bring thee down to this company. Guidance: The interrogation reveals the providence. He is an Egyptian slave (probably acquired on Amalekite raids into Sinai). His master, three days ago when the man fell sick, left him in the field to die — the standard nomadic practice for a slave too weak to keep up. The man names every target of the raid: 'the south of the Cherethites' (Philistine territory), 'the coast belonging to Judah,' 'the south of Caleb' (Judean clans), 'and we burned Ziklag with fire.' He is an eyewitness to the very crime David is pursuing. The detail of his being abandoned is the chapter's quiet theological point: the Amalekites' treatment of their own servants will be repaid by their own servant guiding David back to them. Cruelty to those at the bottom of one's social order has its own boomerang trajectory. David asks if he can guide them; the Egyptian asks for an oath of safety. 'Swear unto me by God, that thou wilt neither kill me, nor deliver me into the hands of my master.' He fears being returned to the Amalekite who left him to die more than anything David might do to him. David swears. The negotiation is conducted with the dignity due any human being whose cooperation must be secured by oath, not extracted by threat. The slave, who has been treated as disposable property, is suddenly treated as a covenant party. Sometimes the LORD arranges for the most despised member of an opposing camp to become the deliverer of His own people.

And when he had brought him down, behold, they were spread abroad upon all the earth, eating and drinking, and dancing, because of all the great spoil that they had taken out of the land of the Philistines, and out of the land of Judah. And David smote them from the twilight even unto the evening of the next day: and there escaped not a man of them, save four hundred young men, which rode upon camels, and fled. Guidance: The Egyptian leads David's four hundred to the camp. The scene is a celebration in progress. The Amalekites are 'spread abroad upon all the earth' — scattered across the entire camp area in a loose, undefended pattern — 'eating and drinking, and dancing, because of all the great spoil.' They have not posted sentries. They believe themselves untraceable; the kind of complacency that comes from having gotten away with the kind of raid that historically goes unanswered. They are wrong. David attacks at twilight — the soft dawn light when an attacking force is hardest to see against the eastern sky. The battle goes 'from the twilight even unto the evening of the next day' — twenty-four hours of running combat across the dispersed camp. The Amalekites cannot organize a counterattack because they are scattered; David rolls them up unit by unit. Final count: every Amalekite dead except four hundred young men who escape on camels (Amalekite camels were used precisely for this kind of fast retreat). The match between the four hundred who fled and the four hundred David brought is a quiet narrative touch. God has not granted total extermination this time; some Amalekites will live to fight another day (Saul's failure in chapter 15 means Amalek remains a problem for the rest of the Old Testament; Haman in Esther is descended from Agag). But the captives — and that is what David came for — are recovered.

And David recovered all that the Amalekites had carried away: and David rescued his two wives. And there was nothing lacking to them, neither small nor great, neither sons nor daughters, neither spoil, nor any thing that they had taken to them: David recovered all. Guidance: Three times in two verses: 'David recovered all… nothing lacking… David recovered all.' The narrator hammers the phrase home because the promise of v8 has been fulfilled with absolute exactness. The LORD said 'without fail recover all' and David has recovered all. Every wife, every child, every animal, every household good. 'Nothing lacking, neither small nor great.' The Hebrew is sweeping: not a single child went missing, not a single household item failed to return. The chapter is showing God's promise-keeping with deliberate precision. When the LORD says 'all,' He means 'all.' 'David rescued his two wives' — Ahinoam and Abigail come home. The personal note is placed in the middle of the collective accounting because David's losses were not different in kind from his men's; the king-to-be experiences God's restoration on the same terms as the rank and file. The whole community will live in the same household tonight that they lived in three days ago. The pattern of recovery is so total it becomes a paradigm — Israelite poets will reference this kind of restoration when describing what the LORD does for His people in their exile-and-return cycles. God is the kind of God who recovers what the enemy took, and the recovery is in the same currency as the loss.

And David recovered all that the Amalekites had carried away: and David rescued his two wives. And there was nothing lacking to them, neither small nor great, neither sons nor daughters, neither spoil, nor any thing that they had taken to them: David recovered all. And David took all the flocks and the herds, which they drave before those other cattle, and said, This is David's spoil. Guidance: There is a bonus. The Amalekites had not just taken Ziklag — their broader raids (per v14) had hit Philistine, Judahite, and Calebite territories. The camp David sacked contained the consolidated loot of all those raids, far exceeding what came from Ziklag alone. David's men round up the surplus flocks and herds and drive them before their own livestock — the larger group of Amalekite-acquired animals in front, David's recovered Ziklag herds behind — and call the surplus 'David's spoil.' The phrase 'David's spoil' marks a category: this is the chief's portion, the prize-of-the-prize, the captain's share. The chapter is going to use this surplus in v26-31 as the source of the diplomatic gifts David sends to the elders of Judah — gifts that will, within months, lead to David's anointing as king of Judah at Hebron (2 Sam 2:4). God has not just restored David's losses; He has given him the political capital to build the next chapter of his life. The Amalekite raid that looked like the end of David has become, through the LORD's hand, the recruiting drive of his coronation. There is a category of providence in which the disaster that nearly destroyed you turns out to have been the funding mechanism for your next assignment.

And David came to the two hundred men, which were so faint that they could not follow David, whom they had made also to abide at the brook Besor: and they went forth to meet David, and to meet the people that were with him: and when David came near to the people, he saluted them. Then answered all the wicked men and men of Belial, of those that went with David, and said, Because they went not with us, we will not give them ought of the spoil that we have recovered, save to every man his wife and his children, that they may lead them away, and depart. Guidance: The returning four hundred reach the brook Besor and the two hundred who were left behind. The two hundred come out to greet them. David 'saluted them' — Hebrew ye'shal lashalom, asked after their peace, treated them as full members of the community. Then the trouble starts. 'All the wicked men and men of Belial' — the chapter labels them sharply — among the four hundred propose: the two hundred get their wives and children back (because David's recovery included them), but they get no share of the additional spoil. The position has a surface fairness — the four hundred actually fought; the two hundred actually rested. It also has a deeper meanness: it is taking advantage of physical weakness to deny brothers what God provided. The proposal is the perfect test of David's leadership. The 'men of Belial' are loud, their argument plausible, and David himself stands to gain politically by siding with the fighting men over the rest-stop guards. What he does in the next verses will define his policy for the rest of his reign.

And David came to the two hundred men, which were so faint that they could not follow David, whom they had made also to abide at the brook Besor: and they went forth to meet David, and to meet the people that were with him: and when David came near to the people, he saluted them. Then answered all the wicked men and men of Belial, of those that went with David, and said, Because they went not with us, we will not give them ought of the spoil that we have recovered, save to every man his wife and his children, that they may lead them away, and depart. Guidance: The returning four hundred reach the brook Besor and the two hundred who were left behind. The two hundred come out to greet them. David 'saluted them' — Hebrew ye'shal lashalom, asked after their peace, treated them as full members of the community. Then the trouble starts. 'All the wicked men and men of Belial' — the chapter labels them sharply — among the four hundred propose: the two hundred get their wives and children back (because David's recovery included them), but they get no share of the additional spoil. The position has a surface fairness — the four hundred actually fought; the two hundred actually rested. It also has a deeper meanness: it is taking advantage of physical weakness to deny brothers what God provided. The proposal is the perfect test of David's leadership. The 'men of Belial' are loud, their argument plausible, and David himself stands to gain politically by siding with the fighting men over the rest-stop guards. What he does in the next verses will define his policy for the rest of his reign.

Then said David, Ye shall not do so, my brethren, with that which the LORD hath given us, who hath preserved us, and delivered the company that came against us into our hand. For who will hearken unto you in this matter? but as his part is that goeth down to the battle, so shall his part be that tarrieth by the stuff: they shall part alike. And it was so from that day forward, that he made it a statute and an ordinance for Israel unto this day. Guidance: David's response is one of his finest moments as a king-in-formation. (1) The address: 'my brethren.' He calls them brothers, even the men of Belial — including them in the same family he is defending the two hundred from being excluded from. (2) The theology: 'that which the LORD hath given us, who hath preserved us.' The spoil is not earned by combat skill; it is given by God for His own reasons. Whoever did or did not fight, the credit belongs to the giver, and the giver decides who eats. (3) The principle: 'as his part is that goeth down to the battle, so shall his part be that tarrieth by the stuff: they shall part alike.' The phrase 'part alike' (yachdav yachloqu) is the standard Hebrew formula for equal division. David formalizes the principle: those who guard the supplies get the same share as those who fight. The reasoning is operational as well as moral — an army with rear-echelon troops as full members of the spoil-economy is a healthier army than one where front-line men hoard the gains. (4) The lasting effect: 'he made it a statute and an ordinance for Israel unto this day.' Written long after the events, the editorial note tells us this principle became the standard policy of David's reign and Israel's military culture for generations. One decision in one wadi shapes the operational ethics of an entire people for a millennium. Great policies often originate in single tests well-handled in obscure places.

And when David came to Ziklag, he sent of the spoil unto the elders of Judah, even to his friends, saying, Behold a present for you of the spoil of the enemies of the LORD; To them which were in Bethel, and to them which were in south Ramoth, and to them which were in Jattir, And to them which were in Aroer, and to them which were in Siphmoth, and to them which were in Eshtemoa, And to them which were in Rachal, and to them which were in the cities of the Jerahmeelites, and to them which were in the cities of the Kenites, And to them which were in Hormah, and to them which were in Chor-ashan, and to them which were in Athach, And to them which were in Hebron, and to all the places where David and his men were wont to haunt. Guidance: The final move of the chapter is political and beautiful. David returns to Ziklag and distributes the surplus 'spoil of the enemies of the LORD' to the elders of thirteen Judean towns. The list is geographically sweeping — Bethel (probably southern Bethuel), south Ramoth, Jattir, Aroer, Siphmoth, Eshtemoa, Rachal, the Jerahmeelite cities, the Kenite cities, Hormah, Chor-ashan, Athach, Hebron. These are the towns David and his band passed through and operated near during the wilderness years. He calls them 'my friends' — they have been part of his protection network for many seasons, perhaps sheltering small contingents, providing food, looking the other way. The gift is a thank-you and an investment. By next year, when Saul falls on Gilboa and David moves to Hebron, these elders will already have an account with him. They will be the ones who anoint him king of Judah at Hebron (2 Sam 2:4). The chapter has shown us a man who, having received from the LORD beyond what he asked, immediately turns the surplus into a generous diplomatic distribution to the political constituency he will need in three months. The line 'where David and his men were wont to haunt' is touching — the wilderness years had a geography of relationships, and David is honoring them all. The phrase 'spoil of the enemies of the LORD' frames the gift theologically: this is not David's loot but the LORD's vindication, distributed by His servant. The chapter ends with the man who began it grieving over a burned town now distributing wealth across his future kingdom. The narrative arc — disaster, self-strengthening in the LORD, inquiry, providence-guided pursuit, total recovery, equitable distribution, political endowment — is one of the cleanest in scripture. The next chapter will tell us what happened on Gilboa while David was recovering at Ziklag. Saul dies. David's kingdom begins. The chapter we have just read is the providence that made him ready.
